title: Google Reportedly Delays Gemini 3.5 Pro to July: Do Not Build Around an Unshipped Model
desc: Multiple reports say Google pushed Gemini 3.5 Pro to July 2026; Google declined to comment. Why operators should not architect around an unshipped model.
keywords: gemini 3.5 pro delay, gemini 3.5 pro release date, google gemini 3.5 pro, gemini 3.5 flash, model agnostic ai, ai automation strategy
cta: Want AI automation that is not hostage to one model's release date?
body:
June 26, 2026. Gemini 3.5 Pro, Google's next flagship model and one of the most anticipated AI releases of the year, looks set to miss its June window. According to multiple reports this week, from Reuters and Business Insider among others, Google has pushed the launch to July to keep refining the model. Google has not confirmed the change, and a spokesperson declined to comment, so treat the date as reported rather than official. The lesson for businesses holds either way.

What is reported

  1. Gemini 3.5 Pro, expected to ship in June after its preview at Google's May developer conference, has reportedly slipped to July 2026.
  2. The reason given is more refinement of the model's coding, token efficiency, and performance on long tasks, based on early tester feedback and real world data.
  3. Gemini 3.5 Flash, the faster and cheaper sibling model, is already available, so Google has a current generation model in market.
  4. Google declined to comment on the revised timeline, so the July date comes from reporting and insiders, not an official announcement.

What it means for operators

Frontier models slip. Gemini 3.5 Pro has been almost here for weeks, and the delay is a useful reminder not to build your roadmap around a model that has not shipped. If a planned automation only works with a specific unreleased model, it is not a plan, it is a wait. The businesses that get results treat the model as a swappable part. Build the workflow now on a model that is available today, since Gemini 3.5 Flash and the current models from OpenAI and Anthropic are more than capable for the large majority of business automation, and upgrade the engine when something better actually ships and proves itself on your task. That model agnostic approach also protects you from the opposite risk, a model you depend on being delayed, deprecated, or pulled, which we saw earlier this month. It is how we build automation for clients through our AI automation and AI automation agency services, and why we tell anyone looking to hire an AI engineer to insist on a design that is not married to one provider. The next great model is always one month away. Your automation should not have to wait for it.
faqs: According to multiple reports this week from outlets including Reuters and Business Insider, Google has pushed Gemini 3.5 Pro from June to July 2026 to keep refining it. Google has not officially confirmed the new date and a spokesperson declined to comment, so it should be treated as reported rather than official.
Why is Google delaying Gemini 3.5 Pro?

The reported reason is further refinement of the model's coding ability, token efficiency, and performance on long, complex tasks, informed by early tester feedback and real world data. The flagship Pro model remains in limited preview while its faster sibling, Gemini 3.5 Flash, is already generally available.
Can I use Gemini now or should I wait?

You do not need to wait. Gemini 3.5 Flash is available today, alongside current models from OpenAI and Anthropic, and these handle the large majority of business automation well. Building now on an available model and upgrading later is almost always better than pausing for an unreleased one.
How do I avoid being hurt by model delays?

Stay model agnostic. Design automations so the underlying model is a swappable component rather than a hard dependency. That way a delayed, deprecated, or withdrawn model is an easy substitution instead of a project stopper, and you can adopt better models as they actually ship.
